articles.earthquake_title: Preparing Your Home and Belongings for Earthquakes: 6 Essential Tips
articles.earthquake_desc: Living in an earthquake-prone area requires more than basic awareness; it demands active steps to ensure the safety of your home and family.
articles.typhoon_title: Prepare Your Home: How to Get Ready for a Typhoon
articles.typhoon_desc: Can you believe we are already in the middle of the year? As we enter the months filled with tropical cyclones and isolated rains, we bid goodbye to our favorite summer activities and destinations.
articles.fire_desc: A fire can threaten lives within just two minutes. A home can burn completely within five minutes.

earthquake.title: Preparing Your Home and Property for an Earthquake: 6 Essential Tips
earthquake.source_info: This content was originally published by GeoVera.
earthquake.intro: Living in an earthquake-prone area requires proactive preparation. Earthquakes strike without warning, but taking these straightforward steps can significantly reduce risks to your home and protect your family.
earthquake.section1_content: Unsecured cabinets can swing open during a quake, spilling their contents. Install safety latches on your cabinet doors to keep everything securely inside and prevent injuries or broken valuables.
earthquake.section2_content: A quick, unobstructed escape is crucial during an emergency. Regularly check the paths to all your doors and windows and keep them completely free of clutter and tripping hazards.
earthquake.section3_content: Teach your family the "Drop, Cover, and Hold On" method and practice drills periodically. Identify safe spots in every room, such as under sturdy furniture or against an interior wall, far away from windows and objects that could topple.
earthquake.section4_content: Tall, heavy items like bookcases, dressers, and shelving units should be securely anchored directly to the walls. Relocate heavy objects to lower shelves, and make sure to secure items like TVs and computer monitors.
earthquake.section5_content: Water heaters, gas fixtures, and other utilities should be anchored and fitted with flexible connectors to prevent dangerous leaks. Store flammable or toxic substances (like cleaning supplies or paint) in low, secure areas where they cannot spill.
earthquake.section6_content: Pay attention to smaller fixtures that can cause damage or break. Secure picture frames, mirrors, and lamps. Organize loose cables to prevent tripping, and consider applying safety film to your windows to prevent glass from shattering.

fire.title: Home Fires: Essential Safety & Prevention Guide
fire.source_info: This content was originally published by Ready.
fire.intro: A fire can become life-threatening in just two minutes, and a residence can be completely engulfed in flames in five. Understanding fire behavior and taking simple preventative steps can save your home and your family.
fire.fire_fast_content: A small flame can turn into a major fire in less than 30 seconds.
fire.fire_hot_content: Heat is more threatening than flames. Room temperatures can reach 600 degrees at eye level, which can instantly scorch your lungs.
fire.fire_dark_content: Fire quickly produces thick black smoke and toxic gases. These gases kill more people than actual flames by causing disorientation and drowsiness.
fire.map_exits_content: Find two ways out of every room in case the primary exit is blocked. Ensure windows and security bars can be opened quickly.
fire.practice_escape_content: Practice your fire escape plan twice a year, including feeling your way out in the dark with your eyes closed.
fire.sleep_safely_content: Always sleep with your bedroom door closed to slow the spread of smoke and heat, and keep a fire extinguisher in the kitchen.
fire.safe_cooking_content: Never leave a frying or broiling stove unattended. Keep outdoor barbecue grills at least 10 feet away from your home's siding and deck railings.
fire.electrical_safety_content: Replace frayed or damaged appliance cords immediately. Never run cords under rugs, and never overload extension cords.
fire.protect_children_content: Store matches and lighters in locked cabinets out of reach. Teach children early on that fire is a tool, not a toy.
fire.during_fire_title: During a Fire: How to Survive
fire.crawl_low_content: Heavy smoke and poisonous gases collect at the ceiling first. Drop to the floor and crawl under the smoke to your exit.
fire.feel_doors_content: Before opening any door, feel the doorknob and the door itself. If either is hot, leave it closed and use your second way out.
fire.if_trapped_content: Close the door and cover vents and door cracks with cloth or tape to keep smoke out. Call emergency services and signal for help from a window using a flashlight or light-colored cloth.
fire.stop_drop_roll_content: If your clothes catch fire, stop immediately, drop to the ground, cover your face with your hands, and roll back and forth to smother the flames.
fire.closing_text: Remember, your best defense against a fire is a working smoke alarm and a practiced escape plan.
fire.closing_text_italic: Stay safe and always prioritize your family's well-being.

typhoon.title: Ready Your Home: How to Prepare for a Typhoon
typhoon.source_info: This content was originally published by Camella.
typhoon.intro: As we enter the wet season, severe tropical cyclones and heavy rains become a regular threat. Protect your property and keep your family safe with this essential typhoon preparedness checklist.
typhoon.inspect_roof_content: Check for holes, cracks, or loose panels. Patch minor issues with sealant and ensure screws are tight to prevent wind damage and leaks.
typhoon.clear_gutters_content: Sweep away leaves and dirt from your gutters. Ensure community street drains are free of garbage to prevent flash flooding.
typhoon.trim_trees_content: Cut back weak branches and overgrown plants to minimize dangerous flying debris during high winds.
typhoon.secure_windows_content: Ensure frames are sturdy and can withstand heavy wind. Bring all outdoor furniture inside.
typhoon.check_wiring_content: Fix any faulty wiring or grounded switches to prevent electric shock during power outages. Have a plumber fix pipe leaks early.
typhoon.stock_pantry_content: Store at least a two-week supply of canned goods and drinking water. Include a manual can opener and high-calorie snacks for your family.
typhoon.emergency_kit_content: Prepare a bag with a first-aid kit, flashlight, batteries, hand-crank radio, and medicines for chronic conditions. Keep it accessible and secured.
typhoon.store_water_content: Keep at least 1 liter of water per person per day for several days. Store extra water in clean containers away from children and pets.
typhoon.protect_documents_content: Scan important documents (birth certificates, insurance papers, property deeds) and store digital copies in cloud storage or with a trusted relative.
typhoon.emergency_numbers_content: Write down the local police, fire department, and disaster hotline numbers. Practice dialing these numbers with your children.
typhoon.plan_evacuation_content: Know your evacuation routes and meeting points. Ensure everyone knows how to contact each other if separated. Keep a printed map and list in your emergency kit.
typhoon.check_loved_ones_content: Designate a trusted relative or friend in another city to contact. Call this person if your family gets separated so everyone knows you're safe.
typhoon.closing_text: Stay safe, stay dry, and always monitor local weather updates.
